content.full_text:
CITY OF SAN JOSÉ, CALIFORNIA
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

151 WEST MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
(408) 277-4454

March 23, 1990

JOAN R. GALLO
City Attorney


KATHRYN E. DONOVAN
General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA     95814

RE:  Campaign Issues

Dear Ms. Donovan:

     I am requesting written advice regarding the use of City
facsimile ("fax") machines to send or receive documents
relating to the campaign activities of City elected officials.
Currently, two Councilmembers are running for the position of
Mayor and one Councilmember is in a contested race for
re-election.

QUESTIONS

     1.  Would the Gov. Code Section 85300 prohibition
against the use of public funds for seeking
elective office be violated if an elected
official receives information connected to his
or her re-election campaign on a City fax
machine?

     2.  Does it make any difference to the answer if
the transmission is solicited or unsolicited?

     3.  May the official send out campaign related
documents via a City fax machine?

     4.  If the receipt of an unsolicited fax violates
the prohibition, how should such fax's be
handled?

KATHRYN E. DONOVAN
RE: Campaign Issues
March 23, 1990
Page Two


DISCUSSION

The issue is whether any of the above uses of a City fax
machine constitutes a prohibited expenditure by a public
official of public funds.

There is no apparent difference between the receipt of
unsolicited information by fax, mail or telephone. Would
receipt of campaign related information by mail or telephone
also violate the prohibition? If the receipt of unsolicited
fax communication violates the prohibition, how should the
receipt be handled?

Because several City officials are currently engaged in
campaign activities, I ask that you provide your response as
soon as possible. Please contact me at (408) 277-4454 if you
need any further information or wish to discuss the matter.

Yours truly,

[signature]
JOAN R. GALLO
City Attorney

sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney requests FPPC guidance on whether use of City fax machines to send or receive campaign-related communications by elected officials violates California Government Code Section 85300's prohibition on using public funds for elective office campaigns, and whether solicitation status affects this analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The document contains the question section but no conclusion or FPPC response. OCR quality is moderate with some character recognition errors (e.g., 'caopaign' for 'campaign', 'loax s' for signature). Document dated March 23, 1990, from Joan R. Gallo, City Attorney of San Jose.

3. May the official send out campaign related documents via a City fax machine?

4. If the receipt of an unsolicited fax violates the prohibition, how should such faxes be handled?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the San Jose City Attorney to the FPPC asking for written advice regarding the permissibility of using City fax machines for campaign communications by elected officials currently engaged in mayoral and council races.
